section.title: The Seven and the Martyrdom of Stephen
section.sectionRationale: A practical crisis over the neglected Grecian widows leads the Twelve to appoint seven Spirit-filled men, freeing the apostles for prayer and the word, and the church surges. One of the seven, Stephen, full of grace and power, provokes fierce opposition, is seized on false charges of speaking against the temple and the law, and is dragged before the Sanhedrin. His long speech retells Israel's story to show that God has always worked outside the holy places and that Israel has always resisted His messengers, climaxing in the charge that they have now betrayed and murdered the Righteous One. Enraged, they stone him, and Stephen dies as his Lord did, seeing the Son of Man and praying for his killers, while a young man named Saul looks on.
section.thesis: The God of glory has never been confined to land or temple but has moved with His people from Abraham to the present, and just as Israel rejected Joseph and Moses and the prophets, so it has now rejected and murdered the Righteous One; yet the risen Christ stands at God's right hand to receive His faithful witness, and Stephen's Christlike death, forgiving his enemies, becomes a seed of the mission that Saul will one day carry to the nations.
section.frameNote: Nine movements: the choosing of the seven; the arrest of Stephen on false charges; his speech tracing God with Abraham, with Joseph, with Moses born and rejected, with Moses the redeemer amid Israel's rebellion, and through tabernacle to temple; his climactic indictment; and his martyrdom, forgiving his killers as Saul watches.

units.label: The choosing of the Seven
units.note: the widows are cared for and the word of God spreads

verses.text: In those days when the disciples were increasing in number, the Grecian Jews among them began to grumble against the Hebraic Jews because their widows were being overlooked in the daily distribution of food.
So the Twelve summoned all the disciples and said, “It is unacceptable for us to neglect the word of God in order to wait on tables.
Therefore, brothers, select from among you seven men confirmed to be full of the Spirit and wisdom. We will assign this responsibility to them
and will devote ourselves to prayer and to the {{diakonia|ministry}} of the word.”

This proposal pleased the whole group. They chose Stephen, a man full of faith and of the Holy Spirit, as well as Philip, Prochorus, Nicanor, Timon, Parmenas, and Nicolas from Antioch, a convert to Judaism.
They presented these seven to the apostles, who prayed and laid their hands on them.
So the word of God continued to spread. The number of disciples in Jerusalem grew rapidly, and a great number of priests became obedient to the faith.

units.label: The arrest of Stephen
units.note: false witnesses charge him against the temple and the law

verses.text: Now Stephen, who was full of grace and power, was performing great wonders and signs among the people.
But resistance arose from what was called the Synagogue of the Freedmen, including Cyrenians, Alexandrians, and men from the provinces of Cilicia and Asia. They disputed with Stephen,
but they could not stand up to his wisdom or the Spirit by whom he spoke.
Then they prompted some men to say, “We heard Stephen speak words of blasphemy against Moses and against God.”
So they stirred up the people, elders, and scribes and confronted Stephen. They seized him and brought him before the Sanhedrin,
where they presented false witnesses who said, “This man never stops speaking against this holy place and against the law.
For we have heard him say that Jesus of Nazareth will destroy this place and change the customs that Moses handed down to us.”
All who were sitting in the Sanhedrin looked intently at Stephen, and they saw that his face was like the face of an angel.

units.label: The God of glory and the call of Abraham
units.note: God appears and promises before there is any land or temple

verses.text: Then the high priest asked Stephen, “Are these charges true?”
And Stephen declared: “Brothers and fathers, listen to me! The God of glory appeared to our father Abraham while he was still in Mesopotamia, before he lived in Haran,
and told him, ‘Leave your country and your kindred and go to the land I will show you.’
So Abraham left the land of the Chaldeans and settled in Haran. After his father died, God brought him out of that place and into this land where you are now living.
He gave him no inheritance here, not even a foot of ground. But God promised to give possession of the land to Abraham and his descendants, even though he did not yet have a child.
God told him that his descendants would be foreigners in a strange land, and that they would be enslaved and mistreated four hundred years.
‘But I will punish the nation that enslaves them,’ God said, ‘and afterward they will come forth and worship Me in this place.’

Then God gave Abraham the covenant of circumcision, and Abraham became the father of Isaac and circumcised him on the eighth day. And Isaac became the father of Jacob, and Jacob of the twelve patriarchs.

units.label: Joseph, rejected by his brothers yet used to save
units.note: God is with the rejected one in a foreign land

verses.text: Because the patriarchs were jealous of Joseph, they sold him as a slave into Egypt. But God was with him
and rescued him from all his troubles. He granted Joseph favor and wisdom in the sight of Pharaoh king of Egypt, who appointed him ruler over Egypt and all his household.
Then famine and great suffering swept across Egypt and Canaan, and our fathers could not find food.
When Jacob heard that there was grain in Egypt, he sent our fathers on their first visit.
On their second visit, Joseph revealed his identity to his brothers, and his family became known to Pharaoh.
Then Joseph sent for his father Jacob and all his relatives, seventy-five in all.
So Jacob went down to Egypt, where he and our fathers died.
Their bones were carried back to Shechem and placed in the tomb that Abraham had bought from the sons of Hamor at Shechem for a price he paid in silver.

units.label: Moses born and rejected by his own
units.note: the appointed deliverer is pushed aside by the people he came to help

verses.text: As the time drew near for God to fulfill His promise to Abraham, our people in Egypt increased greatly in number.
Then another king, who knew nothing of Joseph, arose over Egypt.
He exploited our people and oppressed our fathers, forcing them to abandon their infants so they would die.
At that time Moses was born, and he was beautiful in the sight of God. For three months he was nurtured in his father’s house.
When he was set outside, Pharaoh’s daughter took him and brought him up as her own son.
So Moses was educated in all the wisdom of the Egyptians and was powerful in speech and action.
When Moses was forty years old, he decided to visit his brothers, the children of Israel.
And when he saw one of them being mistreated, Moses went to his defense and avenged him by striking down the Egyptian who was oppressing him.
He assumed his brothers would understand that God was using him to deliver them, but they did not.
The next day he came upon two Israelites who were fighting, and he tried to reconcile them, saying, ‘Men, you are brothers. Why are you mistreating each other?’
But the man who was abusing his neighbor pushed Moses aside and said, ‘Who made you ruler and judge over us?
Do you want to kill me as you killed the Egyptian yesterday?’
At this remark, Moses fled to the land of Midian, where he lived as a foreigner and had two sons.

units.label: Moses the redeemer, and Israel's rebellion
units.note: the rejected one is sent as ruler and redeemer, yet Israel turns to idols

verses.text: After forty years had passed, an angel appeared to Moses in the flames of a burning bush in the desert near Mount Sinai.
When Moses saw it, he marveled at the sight. As he approached to look more closely, the voice of the Lord came to him:
‘I am the God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.’ Moses trembled with fear and did not dare to look.
Then the Lord said to him, ‘Take off your sandals, for the place where you are standing is holy ground.
I have indeed seen the oppression of My people in Egypt. I have heard their groaning and have come down to deliver them. Now come, I will send you back to Egypt.’
This Moses, whom they had rejected with the words, ‘Who made you ruler and judge?’ is the one whom God sent to be their ruler and redeemer through the angel who appeared to him in the bush.
He led them out and performed wonders and signs in the land of Egypt, at the Red Sea, and for forty years in the wilderness.
This is the same Moses who told the Israelites, ‘God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among your brothers.’
He was in the assembly in the wilderness with the angel who spoke to him on Mount Sinai, and with our fathers. And he received living words to pass on to us.
But our fathers refused to obey him. Instead, they rejected him and in their hearts turned back to Egypt.
They said to Aaron, ‘Make us gods who will go before us! As for this Moses who led us out of the land of Egypt, we do not know what has happened to him.’
At that time they made a calf and offered a sacrifice to the idol, rejoicing in the works of their hands.

But God turned away from them and gave them over to the worship of the host of heaven, as it is written in the book of the prophets: ‘Did you bring Me sacrifices and offerings forty years in the wilderness, O house of Israel?
You have taken along the tabernacle of Molech and the star of your god Rephan, the idols you made to worship. Therefore I will send you into exile beyond Babylon.’

units.label: From tabernacle to temple: the Most High is not confined
units.note: the God of heaven and earth does not dwell in houses made by hands

verses.text: Our fathers had the tabernacle of the Testimony with them in the wilderness. It was constructed exactly as God had directed Moses, according to the pattern he had seen.
And our fathers who received it brought it in with Joshua when they dispossessed the nations God drove out before them. It remained until the time of David,
who found favor in the sight of God and asked to provide a dwelling place for the God of Jacob.
But it was Solomon who built the house for Him.
However, the Most High does not dwell in houses made by human hands. As the prophet says:
‘Heaven is My throne and the earth is My footstool. What kind of house will you build for Me, says the Lord, or where will My place of repose be?
Has not My hand made all these things?’

units.label: The indictment: you always resist the Holy Spirit
units.note: as your fathers rejected the messengers, you have betrayed the Righteous One

verses.text: You {{stiffNecked|stiff-necked}} people with uncircumcised hearts and ears! You always resist the Holy Spirit, just as your fathers did.
Which of the prophets did your fathers fail to persecute? They even killed those who foretold the coming of the {{dikaios|Righteous One}}. And now you are His betrayers and murderers,
you who received the law ordained by angels, yet have not kept it.”

units.label: The martyrdom of Stephen
units.note: he sees the Son of Man, forgives his killers, and falls asleep as Saul looks on

verses.text: On hearing this, the members of the Sanhedrin were enraged, and they gnashed their teeth at him.
But Stephen, full of the Holy Spirit, looked intently into heaven and saw the glory of God and Jesus standing at the right hand of God.
“Look,” he said, “I see heaven open and the {{sonOfMan|Son of Man}} standing at the right hand of God.”
At this they covered their ears, cried out in a loud voice, and rushed together at him.
They dragged him out of the city and began to stone him. Meanwhile the witnesses laid their garments at the feet of a young man named Saul.
While they were stoning him, Stephen appealed, “Lord Jesus, receive my spirit.”
Falling on his knees, he cried out in a loud voice, “Lord, do not hold this sin against them.” And when he had said this, he {{koimao|fell asleep}}.

diakonia.gloss: Service, ministry, the practical work of meeting needs; the related verb lies behind waiting on tables. Luke sets the two side by side, the daily service of food and the ministry of the word, and treats both as diakonia, honorable service in the church. The seven are appointed so that neither the widows nor the word is neglected, and from this humble table service Stephen and Philip rise to powerful gospel work.

stiffNecked.gloss: Stiff-necked, obstinate, hard of neck like an ox that will not bend to the yoke. It is the very word God used of Israel at Sinai after the golden calf. Stephen turns their own Scriptures on his judges: the people who accuse him of dishonoring Moses are the true heirs of the wilderness generation, resisting the Spirit and refusing to bow to God's word and His Messiah.

dikaios.gloss: Righteous, just, the Righteous One. Used as a title, it names Jesus as the sinless, wholly just servant whose coming the prophets foretold. Stephen's charge is devastating: the prophets who announced the Righteous One were persecuted, and now the council has betrayed and murdered the Righteous One Himself, adding the greatest sin to the pattern of their fathers.

sonOfMan.gloss: Son of Man, the title Jesus most often used of Himself, drawn from Daniel 7, where one like a son of man comes to the Ancient of Days and receives everlasting dominion. This is the only time in Acts that the title is spoken by someone other than Jesus. Stephen sees the exalted Son of Man standing to receive him, confirming that the crucified Jesus is the enthroned Lord of Daniel's vision.

koimao.gloss: To fall asleep, a tender word the New Testament uses for the death of believers. Stephen is stoned in fury, yet Luke says he fell asleep, for death has been disarmed by the risen Christ into whose hands he commits his spirit. The violent martyrdom is described with the gentleness of rest, because the one who receives him is the Lord of life.

basic.context: The growing church hits a practical problem: the Greek-speaking widows are being overlooked in the daily food distribution. The apostles will not abandon prayer and the word, so seven Spirit-filled men are chosen to oversee the service, and the church keeps growing. One of them, Stephen, does great signs and stirs fierce opposition. False witnesses accuse him of speaking against the temple and the law, and he is dragged before the Sanhedrin. His long reply retells Israel's history, showing that God worked long before there was any temple and that Israel always rejected His chosen messengers. When he charges them with murdering the Righteous One and sees the Son of Man in heaven, they stone him, and he dies forgiving them, watched by a young man named Saul.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the speech as Stephen's answer to two charges, that he attacked the temple and the law. He does not deny God's gifts of law and temple; he shows they were never meant to box God in, and that the real problem is a people who resist the Spirit. The history is not a detour; every stage, Joseph, Moses, the prophets, builds one point: God's people keep rejecting the ones God sends, and now they have done it to Jesus. The martyrdom is deliberately told to echo Jesus' own death.
basic.originalLanguages: A few words carry the section. The seven are set over the diakonia, the service, so the apostles can give themselves to the ministry of the word. Stephen calls his judges stiff-necked, sklerotrachelos, the exact term God used of Israel after the golden calf. He names Jesus the dikaios, the Righteous One, whom they betrayed and murdered. And he sees the Son of Man, the huios tou anthropou of Daniel 7, standing in heaven, before he falls asleep, koimao, the gentle word for a believer's death.
basic.typology: Joseph, rejected by his brothers yet raised to save them, and Moses, pushed aside by his own people yet sent as their ruler and redeemer, both foreshadow Jesus, rejected by Israel yet exalted to save. The prophet like Moses whom God promised is Christ. The tabernacle and temple point beyond themselves to the God who fills heaven and earth and finally to Christ, in whom God dwells with His people.
basic.christ: Jesus is the pattern's climax: the rejected Joseph, the rejected Moses, the prophet like Moses, and above all the Righteous One whom the prophets foretold and this generation murdered. At the end He appears as the Son of Man standing at God's right hand to welcome His witness. Stephen dies as Jesus did, entrusting his spirit to the Lord and praying forgiveness for his killers, so that his death is stamped with the death of Christ.
basic.god: God is the God of glory who appears to Abraham in pagan Mesopotamia, who is with Joseph in Egypt, who speaks to Moses in a desert bush, and who fills heaven and earth so that no house can contain Him. He is faithful to His promises across centuries, patient with a rebellious people, and just in His judgment. He receives His faithful servant into glory even as men rage.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 12 records the call of Abraham that opens the speech. Deuteronomy 18:15 promises the prophet like Moses, fulfilled in Jesus. Isaiah 66:1-2 declares that no house can contain the Most High. Luke 23:34 and 23:46 give Jesus' words that Stephen echoes in his dying prayers.
basic.oneStory: The ascended Christ pours out His Spirit, and the gospel, resisted in Jerusalem, is about to break its bounds. Stephen shows that God was never trapped in the temple and that His people have always rejected His messengers, right up to the Righteous One. His Christlike death is not the end but a seed: scattered by the persecution that follows, the church will carry the word into Judea, Samaria, and beyond, and the very Saul who guards the coats will become the apostle to the nations.

context.literary: This section forms the bridge from the Jerusalem church to the wider mission. The choosing of the seven introduces Stephen and Philip, who drive the next chapters. Stephen's speech is the longest in Acts, a sweeping retelling of Israel's story that answers his accusers and indicts them. His martyrdom closes the Jerusalem phase and introduces Saul, setting up the scattering of the church and the spread of the gospel in chapter 8 and beyond.
context.historical: The Grecian, or Hellenist, Jews were Greek-speaking, often returned from the diaspora, and cultural friction with the Aramaic-speaking Hebraic Jews was real; the widows depended on communal support. The Synagogue of the Freedmen gathered diaspora Jews, likely including men from Saul's Cilicia. The charge of speaking against the temple was explosive in a city whose life revolved around it. Stoning was the prescribed penalty for blasphemy, and the witnesses cast the first stones.
context.canonical: Stephen's speech gathers the whole Old Testament story and reads it as pointing to Christ and exposing Israel's habit of rejecting God's messengers, a theme Jesus Himself pressed. Its temple critique stands with Isaiah 66 and prepares for the New Testament teaching that God now dwells in His people by the Spirit. Stephen, the first Christian martyr, becomes the model of dying like Christ, and his death is the hinge on which Saul's story turns.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: Luke means to show why the gospel began to move beyond Jerusalem, to present Stephen as a faithful, Spirit-filled witness who dies like his Master, and to expose the deep resistance of the leaders to the Spirit and the Righteous One. He also quietly introduces Saul, preparing the reader for the great reversal to come.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The speech is largely a theological reading of history, and its claims about God and about Israel's pattern of rejection are meant to instruct every reader. The appointment of the seven describes a wise, Spirit-led solution to a real need rather than laying down a fixed office, though it models the principle of guarding the ministry of the word while caring practically for the vulnerable. Stephen's forgiving death is held up as an example of Christlikeness.

debates.question: Does Stephen's speech condemn the temple itself, or only its idolatrous misuse?

views.label: It condemns trusting in the temple, not the temple as God's gift
views.case: Stephen affirms that God directed the tabernacle and that Solomon built the house, yet quotes Isaiah 66 that the Most High does not dwell in houses made by hands. The target is the presumption that God can be contained or that possessing the temple secures His favor; the building was good, but making it an idol was not. This fits Jesus, who honored the temple yet foretold its end and spoke of His body as the true temple.

views.label: It announces the temple's obsolescence in God's unfolding plan
views.case: By stressing that God met Abraham, Joseph, and Moses far from Jerusalem, and that heaven and earth cannot house Him, Stephen signals that the temple's era is passing now that Christ has come. The charge that he said Jesus would destroy this place was a distortion of a true expectation: worship is no longer tied to one holy place but to the risen Lord and the Spirit, John 4:21-24.

debates.bearing: Both agree Stephen honors the temple as God's appointed gift while denying it can contain or bind God; they differ on whether his main point is against idolizing the temple or announcing that its central role has now given way to Christ and the Spirit.

study.originalLanguages: Luke dignifies humble service by calling the care of widows a diakonia alongside the ministry of the word, so the seven are no lesser servants. In his climax Stephen reaches for Israel's own vocabulary: they are sklerotrachelos, stiff-necked, the word God spoke over the golden-calf generation, and they have betrayed the dikaios, the Righteous One, foretold by the prophets. Then heaven opens and Stephen sees the huios tou anthropou, the Son of Man of Daniel 7, standing at God's right hand, and having entrusted his spirit to Jesus he koimao, falls asleep, the tender word that turns a brutal stoning into rest.

typology.type: Joseph rejected by his brothers, then exalted to save them
typology.fulfillment: Foreshadows Jesus, rejected by His own yet raised by God to become the Savior of those who rejected Him, Genesis 45, Acts 7:9-14.

typology.type: Moses, refused as ruler and judge, sent as ruler and redeemer
typology.fulfillment: Pictures Christ, rejected by Israel yet appointed by God as ruler and Redeemer; the prophet like Moses of Deuteronomy 18:15 is Jesus.

typology.type: Tabernacle and temple as a copy of the heavenly pattern
typology.fulfillment: Point beyond themselves to the God who fills heaven and earth and to Christ, in whom God dwells and through whom His people become His temple by the Spirit, John 2:19-21, Ephesians 2:21-22.

study.christ: The whole speech drives to Christ. Joseph rejected and raised to save, Moses refused and sent as redeemer, the promised prophet like Moses, and the Righteous One foretold by all the prophets, are gathered up in Jesus, whom this generation has betrayed and murdered. Yet He is not dead but reigning: Stephen sees the Son of Man standing at God's right hand, risen and exalted, ready to receive His servant. Stephen dies as Jesus did, praying Lord Jesus, receive my spirit, and Lord, do not hold this sin against them, so that his martyrdom is a portrait of his crucified and living Lord.
study.god: God is the God of glory, unbound by place, who appears to Abraham in Mesopotamia before any promised land, who is with Joseph in Egypt, who meets Moses in a wilderness bush, and who declares that heaven is His throne and earth His footstool. He keeps His promises across four centuries of slavery, bears long with a rebellious people, and judges their idolatry justly. He is present with His suffering witness, opening heaven to Stephen and welcoming him home. He cannot be domesticated by temple or ritual; He is to be worshiped, obeyed, and trusted.

crossReferences.note: The call of Abraham with which Stephen opens, God appearing and promising before there is any land.
The prophet like Moses whom God will raise up; Stephen cites it, and the New Testament fulfills it in Jesus.
Heaven is My throne and earth My footstool; the Most High is not contained by houses made with hands.
Jesus' own dying words, entrusting His spirit and forgiving His killers, which Stephen deliberately echoes.
One like a son of man comes to the Ancient of Days and receives everlasting dominion; the vision Stephen sees fulfilled in the exalted Jesus.

study.oneStory: The exalted Christ has sent His Spirit, and now the gospel, choked in Jerusalem, is ready to overflow. Stephen shows that God was never confined to land or temple and that His people have long rejected the ones He sends, culminating in the murder of the Righteous One. But the Son of Man reigns and receives His faithful witness, and Stephen's Christlike death, forgiving his enemies, becomes seed. The persecution that follows scatters the church into Judea and Samaria, and the young man guarding the coats, Saul, will be seized by the risen Christ and carry His name to the ends of the earth.

quiz.q: Why did the apostles have the church choose seven men?

options.text: So the Grecian widows would be cared for while the apostles kept devoting themselves to prayer and the ministry of the word.
options.note: Verses 2 to 4 give both the need and the apostles' priority.

options.text: Because the Grecian widows were being overlooked in the daily distribution.
options.note: True, this was the trigger, but the fuller reason includes guarding the ministry of the word.

options.text: Because the apostles no longer wished to serve anyone at tables.
options.note: It was not distaste for service but a matter of calling and priority; both tasks are honored as ministry.

options.text: Because the church needed officials to manage its money and property.
options.note: The concern was caring for widows and preserving the word, not general administration.

quiz.q: What was the thrust of the false charges against Stephen?

options.text: That he never stopped speaking against the temple and the law, saying Jesus would destroy the place and change Moses' customs.
options.note: Verses 13 to 14 give the accusation about the holy place and the law.

options.text: That he had spoken blasphemy against Moses and against God.
options.note: True, from verse 11, but the formal charges before the council centered on the temple and the law.

options.text: That he had led a violent revolt against Rome.
options.note: The charges were religious, about the temple and the law, not political rebellion.

options.text: That he had stolen from the common fund of the church.
options.note: No such charge appears; the accusations concerned the temple and the law.

quiz.q: What is the main point Stephen's retelling of Israel's history makes?

options.text: God has always worked beyond the holy places, and Israel has always resisted the messengers He sends, now even the Righteous One.
options.note: The speech traces God with Abraham, Joseph, and Moses and climaxes in the charge of resisting the Spirit and murdering Jesus.

options.text: God cannot be contained in a temple made by human hands.
options.note: A key strand, from verses 48 to 50, but part of the larger point about resisting God's messengers.

options.text: The law and the temple were always mistakes that God never wanted.
options.note: Stephen affirms God gave the tabernacle pattern and living words; the fault is idolizing them and resisting the Spirit, not the gifts themselves.

options.text: Israel's history proves that God had abandoned His promises to Abraham.
options.note: The opposite; Stephen shows God faithfully keeping His promises across the centuries.

quiz.q: What did Stephen see as he was about to be killed?

options.text: The glory of God and the Son of Man, Jesus, standing at the right hand of God.
options.note: Verses 55 to 56 record his vision of the exalted Christ.

options.text: Heaven opened above the Sanhedrin.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but the heart of the vision was Jesus, the Son of Man, standing at God's right hand.

options.text: An angel coming to carry him bodily out of the city.
options.note: No such rescue is described; he saw the exalted Jesus and was then stoned.

options.text: A vision of the temple restored in splendor.
options.note: He saw the risen Christ in heaven, not a restored temple.

quiz.q: How does Stephen's death mirror the death of Jesus?

options.text: He entrusted his spirit to the Lord and prayed forgiveness for his killers, as Jesus did.
options.note: Verses 59 to 60 echo Jesus' words in Luke 23:46 and 23:34.

options.text: He cried out in a loud voice as he died.
options.note: True, but the deeper likeness is committing his spirit to the Lord and forgiving his enemies.

options.text: He was crucified outside the city like Jesus.
options.note: He was stoned, not crucified, though he did suffer outside the city; the real parallel is his words and spirit.

options.text: He called down judgment on those who stoned him.
options.note: He did the opposite, praying that the sin not be held against them.

quiz.q: Who is introduced at Stephen's stoning, and how?

options.text: Saul, at whose feet the witnesses laid their garments, consenting to the killing.
options.note: Verse 58 introduces Saul guarding the coats, setting up his later story.

options.text: A young man named Saul who was present at the scene.
options.note: True but understated; the witnesses laid their cloaks at his feet, marking his approval of the deed.

options.text: Gamaliel, who tried to stop the stoning.
options.note: Gamaliel appears earlier in Acts 5; the one introduced here is Saul, and he does not intervene.

options.text: Barnabas, who came to defend Stephen.
options.note: Barnabas is not present here; the figure introduced is Saul, who consents to the stoning.

questions: The church solved the widows' crisis by honoring both practical service and the ministry of the word. Where might God be calling you to serve quietly at the tables, and how do you value such service in your own church?

Stephen showed that God met Abraham, Joseph, and Moses far from any holy place. Are you tempted to confine God to certain buildings, rituals, or seasons, and how does His freedom reshape your worship?
Israel repeatedly rejected the messengers God sent. Where do you sense yourself resisting what God is saying through His word or His people, and what would it mean to soften a stiff neck?
Stephen saw the Son of Man standing to receive him in the moment of his suffering. How does the reality of the reigning, welcoming Christ steady you when you face hostility or fear?
Stephen died praying that his killers' sin would not be held against them. Who is hardest for you to forgive, and what would it look like to entrust them, and yourself, to the Lord?
The young man Saul, who approved this death, became the great apostle to the nations. Whom have you written off as beyond God's reach, and how does Saul's story enlarge your hope and your prayers?

translationNotes.note: Grecian Jews and Hebraic Jews render the Hellenists and the Hebrews; the distinction is largely linguistic and cultural, Greek-speaking against Aramaic-speaking believers.
Seventy-five in all follows the Greek Old Testament count for Jacob's household; the Hebrew of Genesis 46 reads seventy. Stephen quotes the Septuagint familiar to his hearers.
The verse compresses two burial traditions, at Shechem and near Hebron; Stephen telescopes the patriarchal accounts, a common summarizing style, without contradicting the fuller record in Genesis.
The quotation of Amos 5:25-27 follows the Greek Old Testament, which reads Rephan where the Hebrew has Kaiwan, and beyond Babylon where Amos says beyond Damascus, updating the place of exile.

The Righteous One translates ho dikaios, a title for the sinless Messiah; the sentence originally ends with a dash before verse 53, rendered here with a comma so the thought flows into you who received the law.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Acts 6 and Acts 7 (all of 6:1-15 and 7:1-60). Footnote markers, line breaks, and poem speaker markers were dropped when building verse text; poem line text was kept and joined with single spaces. The em dash at the end of Acts 7:52 was converted to a comma per the no-dash rule. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
